bins.name: Include storage in hybrid definition
bins.desc: Fluence argues that the ANOPR's definition of hybrid facility should explicitly cover energy storage systems, because FERC's existing large generator interconnection procedures already recognize storage and the same logic applies to large load interconnection.
quotes: the definition of 'hybrid facility' for large loads should also explicitly include energy storage systems.

bins.name: Expedited path for flexible loads
bins.desc: Fluence supports an expedited interconnection pathway for curtailable and flexible large loads, contending that BESS uniquely enables load flexibility and that hybrid facilities including BESS should qualify for faster processing.
quotes: BESS is uniquely positioned to make large loads curtailable and flexible.
Given these highly desired attributes, the interconnection of hybrid facilities that include BESS should be expedited.

bins.name: Storage co-located with load
bins.desc: Fluence supports treating energy storage co-located or electrically adjacent to large loads as qualifying for hybrid facility treatment under the interconnection rules.
quotes: the definition of 'hybrid facility' for large loads should also explicitly include energy storage systems.

bins.name: Flexibility met by adjacent storage
bins.desc: Fluence urges FERC to ensure that large load flexibility commitments can be satisfied by storage resources physically or electrically adjacent to the load, so that BESS can serve as the flexibility mechanism enabling an expedited pathway.
quotes: BESS is uniquely positioned to make large loads curtailable and flexible.
Given these highly desired attributes, the interconnection of hybrid facilities that include BESS should be expedited.
The Commission should ensure that load commitments to flexibility can be met by resources physically and/or electrically adjacent to load.

bins.name: Proximate resource satisfies flexibility
bins.desc: Fluence asks FERC to allow flexibility commitments to be met by resources adjacent to load, not requiring on-site generation, which aligns with a proximate resource principle.
quotes: The Commission should ensure that load commitments to flexibility can be met by resources physically and/or electrically adjacent to load.

bins.name: Reasonable study assumptions for BESS
bins.desc: Fluence argues that study assumptions for BESS charging should not trigger upgrade requirements unnecessarily, and that overly conservative assumptions extend study timelines and undermine the benefits BESS provides to interconnection queues.
quotes: the Commission should ensure any final action allows storage resources to charge will not trigger the need for upgrades.

If unreasonable assumptions are applied to BESS that result in unnecessary and lengthy study processes, it will limit large loads from benefiting from the optionality that BESS can provide, which will ultimately exacerbate interconnection delays as opposed to quickening the pace of interconnection.

bins.name: Avoid unnecessary upgrade costs
bins.desc: Fluence contends that the final rule should allow storage to charge without triggering network upgrade requirements, reducing interconnection costs for large loads and avoiding delays that undermine the value of BESS.
quotes: the Commission should ensure any final action allows storage resources to charge will not trigger the need for upgrades.

If unreasonable assumptions are applied to BESS that result in unnecessary and lengthy study processes, it will limit large loads from benefiting from the optionality that BESS can provide, which will ultimately exacerbate interconnection delays as opposed to quickening the pace of interconnection.

bins.name: CAISO inverter cap on regulation
bins.desc: Fluence opposes CAISO's rule that caps a BESS resource's regulation capacity at its inverter rating, arguing this leaves up to half of the resource's regulation capability unused and harms both resource economics and system efficiency.
quotes: By artificially restricting regulation capacity to equal the inverter rating, CAISO is leaving 50% of the BESS resource's regulation capability unutilized, harming both resource economics and system efficiency.

bins.name: MISO transmission charges on charging
bins.desc: Fluence opposes MISO's practice of assessing transmission service charges on storage withdrawals, which it says conflicts with Order 841, creates a unique competitive disadvantage versus other regions, and deters storage development where it is most needed.
quotes: MISO's practice of charging transmission service for energy storage withdrawals represents a significant barrier to storage development and is potentially in conflict with Order 841's (specifically 841a) directive regarding comparable treatment of energy storage.

No other RTO/ISO applies transmission charges to storage charging in the manner MISO does, creating a significant competitive disadvantage for MISO and a barrier to IPP-based storage development in the region.

bins.name: Fix market rules for BESS
bins.desc: Fluence argues that RTOs and ISOs impose overly conservative operational restrictions and inadequate bidding frameworks on BESS, including denial of multi-segment bids for ancillary services and administrative SOC management, and that market design failures rather than technology failures are the root cause of observed problems. FERC should require all products to be biddable and eliminate artificial limitations.
quotes: BESS resources require multi-segment bidding capability for all market products, not just energy.
RTOs/ISOs are, at times, imposing overly conservative operational restrictions on BESS resources that prevent these assets from providing their full technical capability to the grid.

By artificially restricting regulation capacity to equal the inverter rating, CAISO is leaving 50% of the BESS resource's regulation capability unutilized, harming both resource economics and system efficiency.
When market design inadequacies create operational problems, the response should not be to limit the technology but to fix the market design.

bins.name: Remove MISO storage charging fees
bins.desc: Fluence asks FERC to revisit MISO's transmission charge on storage withdrawals and issue guidance that storage resources responding to dispatch should not face such charges, consistent with Order 841.
quotes: MISO's practice of charging transmission service for energy storage withdrawals represents a significant barrier to storage development and is potentially in conflict with Order 841's (specifically 841a) directive regarding comparable treatment of energy storage.

No other RTO/ISO applies transmission charges to storage charging in the manner MISO does, creating a significant competitive disadvantage for MISO and a barrier to IPP-based storage development in the region.

bins.name: Allow dual-use transmission storage
bins.desc: Fluence argues that storage serving a transmission function should be permitted to participate in wholesale markets during non-constraint hours, because transmission constraints occur only a fraction of the year and leaving assets idle wastes capacity, suppresses investment, and makes storage-based transmission solutions less competitive than traditional wire upgrades.
quotes: A storage facility sized to address a transmission constraint may only be needed for that purpose during a few hundred hours per year, or in some cases only a few dozen hours.

unless the Commission directs RTOs/ISOs to allow for energy storage resources to provide both whole market and transmission services, we will be limited in how much value we are able to derive from storage as a transmission asset.

bins.name: Cybersecurity in BESS supply chain
bins.desc: Fluence urges FERC to require that cybersecurity be embedded throughout project development and procurement for large load infrastructure, and to ensure that supply chains do not include controls hardware or software from foreign adversaries.
quotes: Cybersecurity should be embedded into every stage of project development and procurement given the sensitivity of large electric loads.
